gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aimed to develop and validate a targeted next-generation sequencing (NGS) method (Targeted DNA-HiSeq) for identifying sequence variants in genetic disease-causing genes. The problem was that traditional Sanger sequencing is laborious, expensive, and time-consuming for analyzing multiple genes, while whole-genome NGS is costly. The solution combined custom array-based capture of 193 genes (3382 exons) with Illumina HiSeq2000 sequencing, using sample barcoding for multiplexing. The method was tested on 7 patients with 6 genetic diseases and 100 controls, with validation by Sanger sequencing and real-time PCR. Key findings: high coverage (99.54% of target at ≥30×), 96.8% sensitivity vs. dbYH, identification of 6 known and 2 novel mutations plus one exon deletion, all confirmed. Limitations included GC-content bias affecting coverage, false positives from incomplete polymorphism database, and inability to detect large rearrangements.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Summarizes the development of Targeted DNA-HiSeq: array-based capture of 193 genes, sequencing of 7 patients and 100 controls, achieving 99.14% exon coverage at >30×, identifying 6 known and 2 novel mutations plus one deletion, all confirmed by Sanger or real-time PCR. Concludes high sensitivity, fidelity, throughput, and speed.
process_signals: Problem: need for efficient, low-cost targeted sequencing of disease genes.
Method: combined enrichment and NGS.
Key result: high coverage and variant detection accuracy.
Validation: Sanger and real-time PCR confirmation.
metrics: 6 known variants, 2 novel mutations, 1 exon deletion

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Genetic disorders require extensive re-sequencing. Sanger sequencing is gold standard but not scalable. NGS is high-throughput but costly for whole genomes. Targeted enrichment with NGS is attractive. This study designed a custom array for 193 genes, used HiSeq2000 with barcoding, and validated with Sanger and MOLDI-TOF-MS.
process_signals: Hypothesis: targeted NGS can detect variants with high fidelity, throughput, speed, and low cost.
Design choice: custom solid-phase array (NimbleGen) for 193 genes.
Validation plan: Sanger and MOLDI-TOF-MS.

section_gists.heading: Patients and controls
section_gists.gist: 107 blood samples: 7 patients with 6 diseases (HSP-TCC, XLI, MFS, Wilson, DMD, β-thalassemia) and 100 normal controls. Additional samples: YH DNA (C1), one normal adult (C2), mother of DMD proband (C3). Informed consent and ethics approval obtained.
process_signals: Problem scope: test on diverse monogenic diseases.
Controls: 100 normal adults to build polymorphism database.
Additional samples for validation (C1 for accuracy, C2/C3 for DMD deletion).

section_gists.heading: Capture array design, library construction and next-generation sequencing
section_gists.gist: Custom NimbleGen array designed to capture 3382 exons, splice sites, adjacent introns of 193 genes (hereditary diseases, arrhythmias, cardiomyopathies, familial cancers). DNA fragmented to 200-300bp, end-repaired, A-tailed, ligated with Illumina adapters with 8bp barcodes. 10 libraries pooled, hybridized 72h, washed, eluted. Paired-end 90bp sequencing on HiSeq2000.
process_signals: Design: targeted 193 genes based on GeneReviews, focusing on Chinese population incidence.
Barcoding: allowed multiplexing of 10 samples per lane.
Protocol: followed standard with minor modifications.
methods: End repair, A-tailing, adapter ligation (Illumina single-read adapters)

section_gists.heading: Data filtering and analysis pipeline
section_gists.gist: Illumina Pipeline v1.3.4 for base calling. Index reads identified (perfect match or ≤3 mismatches). Low-quality reads removed ( >10% Ns, >50% bases with Q<5, average Q<10, adapter contamination). Clean reads aligned with BWA to hg19. SNPs called with SOAPsnp, indels with GATK Indel Genotyper. Known variants from dbSNP, HapMap, HGMD.
process_signals: Filtering: stringent quality control to ensure reliable variant calling.
Tools: BWA, SOAPsnp, GATK.
Databases: dbSNP, HapMap, HGMD for annotation.

section_gists.heading: Sanger sequencing
section_gists.gist: Validation method: PCR amplification of target sites with specific primers, Sanger sequencing using BigDye Terminator v1.1 on ABI 3730 DNA Analyzer.
process_signals: Orthogonal validation to confirm NGS variants.

section_gists.heading: Real-time PCR
section_gists.gist: Quantitative real-time PCR for DMD exon copy number using SYBR green, β-actin as control. ΔΔCt method, fold change calculated. SIFT used for pathogenicity prediction of novel missense variants.
process_signals: Orthogonal validation for copy number variant (exon deletion).
SIFT prediction to assess novel variants.

section_gists.heading: Sequence enrichment, high-throughput sequencing, mapping and coverage
section_gists.gist: High-quality reads per patient: 5.98M–15.19M. 83.74% uniquely mapped to target. 99.54% of target covered at ≥30×. Average exon depth 410×, max 1000×. Coverage adequate for variant detection.
process_signals: Observation: high enrichment efficiency and coverage.
Metric: 99.54% target coverage at ≥30×.

section_gists.heading: Estimation of the accuracy of Targeted DNA-HiSeq
section_gists.gist: Accuracy assessed by comparing YH DNA (C1) variants with dbYH. Initial criteria: ≥10 reads, >14% variant reads, Q>20. Found 1301 SNVs, 95.70% in dbYH. Heterozygous ratio analysis suggested >20% variant reads threshold. Revised criteria: ≥10 reads, >20% variant reads, Q>20. Then 1281 SNVs, 96.8% (1240/1281) in dbYH. 41 C1-only variants (potential false positives).
process_signals: Initial variant calling criteria led to 95.70% concordance.
Refinement: increased heterozygous threshold from >14% to >20% based on distribution analysis, improving concordance to 96.8%.
False positive rate: 3.2% (41/1281) variants not in dbYH.
methods: Comparison with dbYH whole-genome sequencing data
Variant calling criteria: ≥10 reads, >20% variant reads for het, Q>20
failures_or_limits: 41 variants only in C1, possibly false positives or true variants missed by dbYH.

section_gists.heading: Identification of sequence variants
section_gists.gist: In 7 patients, 6317 variants (5734 SNVs, 583 indels). 4841 known SNVs. Filtering: selected HGMD/LSMD variants (155 known disease-causing), 893 novel variants. Further filtered: 44 novel truncating/amino acid altering, 9 candidate indels in coding/splice sites. Built polymorphism database from 100 controls (allele frequency >5% excluded). Final candidate mutations: 79 (29 known, 41 novel, 9 indels). 7 mutations in 6 disease-causing genes (Table 2). 72 additional mutations in 49 other genes.
process_signals: Filtering strategy: known disease databases → novel functional variants → control polymorphism database to remove common variants.
Observation: many potential mutations in other genes, suggesting incomplete polymorphism database or additional disease associations.
methods: Variant filtering: dbSNP, HGMD, LSMD, 1000 Genomes
Custom polymorphism database from 100 controls (AF>5% excluded)
failures_or_limits: 72 potential mutations in other genes not related to diagnosed disease, indicating false positives or incomplete control database.

section_gists.heading: Confirmation of the identified variants by Sanger sequencing in 7 samples
section_gists.gist: All 7 mutations confirmed by Sanger sequencing (100% concordance). Details: P1 (SPG11 insT), P2 (STS G367R novel), P3 (FBN1 C59R novel), P4 (ATP7B R778L and delT), P5 (HBB delTTCT), P6 (HBB IVS2+654C>T), P7 (DMD exon 1 deletion). Deletion validated by real-time PCR: P7 RA~0, mother C3 RA~0.5.
process_signals: Validation success: 100% concordance with Sanger.
Deletion detection: coverage drop in exon 1, confirmed by real-time PCR.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Targeted DNA-HiSeq achieved max FPV 3.2%, avg depth >400×, 99.54% coverage. 72 additional potential mutations found, suggesting incomplete polymorphism database; plan to increase controls. GC content bias: exons with GC>75% or <25% had depth <30×; only 14% of exons with GC>80% captured well. 29 exons consistently low coverage, 19 due to extreme GC. PCR amplification efficiency likely cause. Method cannot detect large rearrangements. Future: larger control database, improve capture for extreme GC regions.
process_signals: Limitation: false positives due to small control database.
Limitation: GC bias reduces coverage in extreme GC regions, affecting variant detection.
Limitation: cannot detect large structural variants.
Future work: increase control samples, optimize capture for GC-rich regions.
metrics: 29 exons with median depth <30× across samples
failures_or_limits: 72 potential false positive mutations in other genes
GC content bias: 36% of extreme GC exons had depth <30×

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The study progressed from problem definition (need for scalable genetic testing) to method development (custom capture array + NGS), then to validation (accuracy estimation using YH DNA, variant identification in patients, and confirmation by orthogonal methods).
Initial variant calling criteria were refined based on heterozygous ratio analysis, improving specificity.

The method successfully identified known and novel mutations, but revealed limitations in GC-rich regions and false positives due to incomplete control databases, prompting plans for future improvements.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Whether the 41 C1-only variants are true false positives or variants missed by dbYH.
The clinical significance of the 72 additional potential mutations in other genes.
The exact sensitivity for indel detection was not quantified separately.
The impact of PCR duplicates on variant calling accuracy was not discussed.

evidence_bank.quote: When multiple genes need to be analyzed for mutations simultaneously, the gathering of information becomes laborious, expensive and time consuming using the Sanger method.
Although NGS technology can be used to analyze gene variants with high throughput screening, its high cost still prevents its application in whole human genome resequencing projects.
we have designed a custom high-density, solid-phase array (Roche NimbleGen, Inc., Madison, WI, USA) to capture 193 distinct genes involved in 103 different genetic diseases.
the DNA adapter-ligated and indexed fragments from 10 libraries were pooled and hybridized to the capture array for 72 h.
we selected 7 samples from seven patients with six different genetic diseases resulting from six disease-causing genes and 100 samples from normal human adults as controls.
99.54% of the targeted region was covered in at least a 30-fold depth in each sample
The average coverage depth for exons was 410-fold, and the highest coverage depth for exons was 1000-fold
we used the Sanger DNA sequencing method followed by PCR amplification and MOLDI-TOF-MS to confirm the results obtained from NGS.
we found 1301 SNVs of 193 genes in YH DNA using Targeted DNA-HiSeq, of which 95.70% (1245/1301) were also identified in dbYH

We finally identified 6317 variants, including 583 indels and 5734 substitutes.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Develop and validate a targeted NGS method for efficient, low-cost identification of sequence variants in genetic disease-causing genes, overcoming limitations of Sanger sequencing and whole-genome NGS.

canonical_events.summary: Sanger sequencing is laborious, expensive, and time-consuming for analyzing multiple genes; whole-genome NGS is too costly for routine resequencing.
Designed custom NimbleGen solid-phase capture array targeting 193 genes (3382 exons) involved in 103 genetic diseases, with sample barcoding for multiplexing 10 libraries per lane.
Sequenced 7 patient samples (6 diseases) and 100 normal controls using Targeted DNA-HiSeq (Illumina HiSeq2000, paired-end 90bp).
Achieved high enrichment and coverage: 83.74% uniquely mapped reads, 99.54% target covered at ≥30×, average exon depth 410×.
Initial accuracy estimation using YH DNA: 95.70% concordance with dbYH (1245/1301 SNVs) using criteria: ≥10 reads, >14% variant reads, Q>20.
Revised heterozygous threshold to >20% based on variant ratio distribution, improving concordance to 96.8% (1240/1281 SNVs).
Variant calling in 7 patients identified 6317 variants (5734 SNVs, 583 indels) using BWA, SOAPsnp, GATK.
Filtering strategy: known disease databases → novel functional variants → custom control polymorphism database (AF>5% excluded), yielding 79 candidate mutations (29 known, 41 novel, 9 indels).

Identified 7 disease-causing mutations in 6 genes: 6 known (SPG11 insT, ATP7B R778L/delT, HBB delTTCT, HBB IVS2+654C>T) and 2 novel (STS G367R, FBN1 C59R), plus DMD exon 1 deletion detected by read depth.
Sanger sequencing confirmed all 7 mutations with 100% concordance; DMD exon 1 deletion validated by real-time PCR (P7 RA~0, mother C3 RA~0.5).
72 additional candidate mutations in 49 other genes suggest incomplete polymorphism database, leading to potential false positives (avg 13.1 per patient).
GC content bias reduces coverage: 36% of exons with GC>75% or <25% had depth <30×; only 14% of exons with GC>80% captured well; 29 exons consistently low coverage.
PCR amplification efficiency likely cause of GC bias, affecting both capture and sequencing.
Array-based hybridization is time-consuming (65-72h) and less flexible than solution-based methods; technological complexity hinders optimization.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Array-based capture method used successfully but with practical drawbacks.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The solid-phase array hybridization process is time-consuming and inflexible.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Array-based hybridization is slower and less adaptable than solution-based methods.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note the limitation of array-based hybridization for clinical and optimization purposes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hybridization to solid-phase array takes 65-72 hours, slower than solution-based (~24h); array redesign is required for probe changes, hindering optimization.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitation for future method development.

evidence.quote_or_span: Hybridization to a solid-phase array takes almost three days (65–72 hours) and is a time-consuming process, making array-based hybridization technology slightly slower for clinical diagnoses compared with solution-based approaches, which require approximately 24 hours.
array-based methods are hampered with regard to further optimization due to their technological complexity. If a probe has to be added or changed, the whole array must be synthesized de novo.

success_verification.final_conclusion: Targeted DNA-HiSeq successfully identified 7 disease-causing mutations with high accuracy, but false positives from incomplete polymorphism database, GC bias, and array-based hybridization limitations remain.
limitations: Incomplete polymorphism database leads to false positives (72 additional candidates)
GC content bias reduces coverage in extreme GC exons (36% with depth <30×)
Array-based hybridization is time-consuming (65-72h) and less flexible
